coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots as nanoprobe for determination of lidocaine hydrochloride
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Gao, Zhiyong
Zhang, Weiqun
Wang, Wei
Chang, Jiuli
Kai, Jiang
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of an easily available fish scales precursor. The as-prepared CDs exhibit good water-solubility, wavelength-tunable fluorescence nature with fluorescence quantum yield of 9%, high photostability and resistance against common ions and biomolecules, thus enables the application as viable fluorescent nanoprobes for detection of pharmaceuticals molecules. The fluorescence of CDs can be efficiently quenched by lidocaine hydrochloride (LH) through static quenching, hence the CDs can serve as a simple and rapid fluorescent probe for determination of LH pharmaceutical. The fluorescent CDs probe demonstrated a wide linear range (0.185–1.295 mmol L−1) and a low detection limit (0.054 mmol L−1). The CDs also demonstrates low cytotoxicity, which enables the accurate fluorescent detection of LH in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fish scales
Lidocaine hydrochloride
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of LH in serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Innovative Research Team and Individuals in University of Henan Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by NSFC (No. 21671059), Program for Innovative Research Team and Individuals in University of Henan Province (Nos. 18 IRTSTHN003 and 15HASTIT006 ) and the 111 Project (No. D17007 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration on the preparation of CDs from fish scales, (b) TEM and HRTEM (inset), (C) XRD and (d) FTIR of CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s spectra of CDs.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under excitation wavelengths at (a) 360 and 700 nm, (b) 220–290 nm, (c) 300–520 and (d) 530–750 nm. CDs concentration: 250 μg mL−1.
PL spectra of aqueous CDs solutions at different concentrations excited at (a) 360 nm and (b) 700 nm. Inset: peak PL intensity as a function of CDs concentration.

PL intensity of CDs solution in presence of (a) inorganic ions (100 μmol L−1), (b) biomolecules (2.5 μg L−1) and PL spectra within pH range of 2–11 at excitation wavelength of (c) 360 nm and (d) 700 nm. CDs concentration: 125 μg mL−1.
Effect of the CDs concentration on PL intensity of CDs-LH system in BR buffer solution at pH = 7.0. CDs concentration: 125 μg mL−1, LH concentration: 0.185 mol.
Effect of (a) buffer solution type and (b) dosage of BR buffer solution on the PL intensity of CDs-LH system. CDs concentration: 125 μg mL−1, LH concentration: 0.185 mM.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs-LH system at various LH concentrations and (b) linear relationship between △F and LH concentration.
Stern-Volmer curves and logarithmic plots of CDs-LH at 298 K. CDs concentration: 125 μg mL−1; BR buffer solution, pH = 7.0.
PC-12 cells livabilities incubated at different CDs concentrations by MTT assay.
Relative errors in PL intensities of CDs-LH system aroused by interferential foreign substances.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Conditions: CDs concentration: 125 μg mL−1; LH: 0.185 mM; BR buffer solution: pH = 7.0.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots as nanoprobe for determination of lidocaine hydrochloride

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Henan, Xinxiang, 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Laboratory Medicine, Xinxiang Medical University, Henan, Xinxiang, 453003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Third Affiliated Hospital of Xinxiang Medical University, Henan, Xinxiang, 453000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Third Affiliated Hospital of Xinxiang Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Henan Normal University, Henan, Xinxiang, 453007, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Henan, Xinxiang, 453007, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of an easily available fish scales precursor. The as-prepared CDs exhibit good water-solubility, wavelength-tunable fluorescence nature with fluorescence quantum yield of 9%, high photostability and resistance against common ions and biomolecules, thus enables the application as viable fluorescent nanoprobes for detection of pharmaceuticals molecules. The fluorescence of CDs can be efficiently quenched by lidocaine hydrochloride (LH) through static quenching, hence the CDs can serve as a simple and rapid fluorescent probe for determination of LH pharmaceutical. The fluorescent CDs probe demonstrated a wide linear range (0.185–1.295 mmol L−1) and a low detection limit (0.054 mmol L−1). The CDs also demonstrates low cytotoxicity, which enables the accurate fluorescent detection of LH in human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Lidocaine hydrochloride (2-(diethylamino)-N-(2,6-dimethylphenyl)-acetamide hydrochloride, LH, Fig. 1 ) is a common amide typed drug with high efficacy in local anesthesia and antiarrhythmia [1,2]. When LH is injected into veins or absorbed by blood and muscle, it inhibits the permeation of sodium ions across the cell membrane and further inhibits the generation and propagation of action potential [3,4], thus leads to the sensory paralysis and mitigates the arrhythmia. However, many clinical reports have warned that the excessive dose of LH also causes toxicity and side effect to heart and nervous system, hence, the sensitive and reliable monitor of LH is crucial for clinical purpose. The current strategies for detection of LH mainly includes high-performance liquid-chromatography (HPLC) [5,6] and capillary electrophoresis coupled with different detectors [7,8]. The chromatography technique is time consuming and need complex instruments, whereas the capillary electrophoresis is non-ideal in stability and reproducibility, so it is imperative to develop cost effective analysis method for quantitative detection of LH.

Fluorescence probe is an alternative strategy for quantitatively determination of analyte with high sensitivity and spatiotemporal resolution. Various analytes, such as metal ions [9–11], pesticides [12], and pollution molecules [13,14], as well as biomolecules [15,16] can be determined by fluorescence probe technique through quenching the fluorescence of fluorophor by electrons transfer to the acceptor, or restoring the fluorescence of fluorophor by compete reaction via an “on/off” manner. However, fluorescent probes techniques for detection of LH molecule have rarely been recorded so far, so the building of efficient fluorescent probe for LH detection is meaningful. Moreover, the fluorescent molecules and semiconductor quantum dots fluorophors often have to confront with either intrinsic difficulty in synthesis or elemental toxicity. Consequently, it is still essential to seek for easily available and reliable fluorescent probes for LH detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) represent a new type of 0 dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials with the doping of heteroatoms or the decoration of functionalities within/onto carbonaceous motif. Since the first discovery in 2004 [17], the last decade have witnessed the flourish of CDs in multimode bioimaging, sensing, photochemical energy storage and catalysis. Thanks to the excellent photostability, tunable emission wavelength, good water-solubility and low elemental toxicity, CDs have been widely employed as alternative fluorescent nanoprobes for quantitative determination of the analytes previously accomplished by fluorescent dyes or semiconductors quantum dots. The preparation of CDs can be categorized into “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches, the former includes the physical [17–19], chemical [20] and electrochemical [21] ablation of carbon motif such as graphite, carbon nanotubes and carbon nanofibers. “Bottom-up” avenue can more easily generate CDs through the oxygenation polymerization, and thermally carbonization of organics and polymers [22]. Moreover, a wide range of easily available natural biomass, such as grass [23], vegetables [24,25], fruit juices [26,27] and even silk protein [28] can be employed as precursor for generation of fluorescent CDs by thermal treatment. Fish scale is a commonly discarded biomass containing rich collagen proteins and crude fat, just as silk proteins, it is also a potential precursor for preparation of CDs, and the application as fluorescent nanoprobe for determination of LH is also expectable.

In this work, fish scale of grass carp was attempted as precursor for preparation of CDs, fortunately, water-soluble fluorescent CDs with high resistance to photobleaching, ion strength, iorganic ions and biomolecules were yielded by a facile hydrothermal treatment. The fluorescence of the CDs can be efficiently quenched by LH via static quenching mode, hence can serve as fluorescent nanoprobe for determination of LH. The CDs based fluorescent probe exhibited a linear range within 0.185–1.295 mmol L−1 and a detection limit of 0.054 mmol L−1, and the high recovery in spiked standard sample shows the potential for LH detection in clinical tests.

LH was commercially obtained from Yao Pharma Co., Ltd (Chongqing, China). Aqueous LH stock solution (20 mg mL−1) was prepared and then kept at 4 °C in the dark. Common inorganic ions solutions such as CaCl2, ZnCl2 and CuSO4, etc, as well as biological molecules such as amino acids and DNA were prepared using deionized water as solvent. All other reagents were of analytical grade and were directly used as received. A series of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions with pH range of 2–11 were prepared to evaluate the fluorescent feature under different media acidity.

Grass carp was purchased from the local supermarket (Xinxiang, China), the fish scales were scraped, dried and smashed into powder, which was subjected to hydrothermal treatment to yield water soluble CDs. In brief, 1.5 g of fish scale powder was ultrasonically dispersed in 80 mL of deionized water and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 20 h. After being cooled naturally, the brown dispersion was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min to remove the sediment. The yielded supernatant was then transferred into dialysis bags (molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da) for dialysis treatment against deionized water for two days. The purified CDs solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane filter and freeze dried. The afforded dark grown CDs sample is highly hydrophilic and can easily adsorb moisture to form thick slurry. An aqueous CDs suspension with concentration of 5 mg mL−1 was prepared for dilution treatment in the subsequent spectroscopic characterizations and tests.

The size and microstructure of the CDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electronic microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM 2100). The structural and chemical bonding information are characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 diffractometer with Cu Kα incident radiation), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet FTS NEXUS, the sample was pressed in KBr pellet) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250, Thermo, UK).

UV-vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the aqueous CDs solution were recorded on UV-1700 UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Co., Japan) and FP-6500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard (QY = 0.54) [29]. Fluorescence lifetime of CDs solution was measured using a FlS-980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK).

Unless specified otherwise, the basic test conditions for determination of LH were as follows: CDs stock dispersion (5 mg mL−1) and a given volume of LH standard stock solution were rapidly mixed in a 10 mL test tube, followed by the addition of 2.5 mL of BR buffer solution to maintain a fixed pH of 7.0. Then, the mixture solution was diluted to 10 mL to afford CDs-LH system with CDs concentration fixed as 125 μg mL−1 and the concentration of LH varies from 0.185 to 1.295 mmol L−1. After stirring for 5 min, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength (λex) of 360 nm with slit width of 10 nm. The interference trials of inorganic ions and biomolecules were performed by monitoring the variation of fluorescence emission intensities of CDs in the presence of these interferential substances under λex of 360 nm.

PC-12 cell (pheochromocytoma of the rat adrenal medulla) was employed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs, in brief, PC-12 cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) (Gibco, USA) containing 5% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Gibco, USA) under 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. The concentration of the harvested PC-12 cells was adjusted to be 5 × 105 cells per well, which were placed in 96 well microtiter plates and incubated with addition of CDs suspensions (100 μL per well) at various concentrations (20.0, 40.0, 100.0, 200.0, and 400.0 μg mL−1). After incubation in humidified CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h, the viable cells concentrations were determined by MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2-2thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide) assay.

Fish scale containing rich collagen protein and other nutritional ingredients, hence can serve as C and N source for preparation of N doped carbons with excellent electrochemical performance in lithium-sulfur batteries and supercapacitors [30,31]. Considering that a wide range of biomass can turn into CDs by controlled thermal treatment, fish scale was attempted as precursor for preparation of CDs, the synthesis procedure was summarized in Fig. 2 a. Undergoes hydrothermal treatment, the peptide chains experience partial hydrolytic cleavage, dehydrogenation and dehydroxylation to afford reactive radical intermediates, which incline to condensation to afford aromatic carbon framework [32], as intuitively evidenced from the brownish color of CDs solution. Moreover, the doping and decoration of N and O functionalities endow the CDs with high hydrophilicity, some double bond containing functionalities even offer fluorescent feature [33], hence the CDs can be a water soluble fluorescent nanomaterial. Under irradiation of ultraviolet light with wavelength centering around 360 nm, the CDs solution demonstrates bluish color (Fig. 2a), manifesting the fluorescent feature of CDs.

Fig. 2b presents the TEM image of the afforded CDs, which demonstrate well separated nanodots with size ranges from 4 to 9 nm, indicating the successful formation of CDs. HRTEM image (inset) of CDs demonstrates well-defined lattice fringes with spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) facets of aromatic carbon framework, implying the formation of graphitic micro-regions within in CDs by hydrothermal carbonization. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2c) of solid sample of CDs exhibit a broad peak at 18.5°, which can be indexed to the reflection of (002) planes of graphitic carbon with interlayer spacing of 0.48 nm. The apparently larger interlayer spacing relative to graphite (0.34 nm) is mainly due to the less effective aromatic stacking of graphitic carbon layers as a result of the abundant N, O functionalities onto CDs [34,35].

Fig. 2d shows the FTIR spectrum of CDs. The characteristic vibration bands around 3400 cm−1 (OH stretching vibration), 3400–3069 cm−1 (NH2 and CONH2 stretching vibrations) and 2955 cm−1 (CH3) indicate the coexistence of −OH, −NH2 and −CH3 functionalities in the CDs [36,37], the absorption peaks at 1637, 1580 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 could be assigned to the CO, CC stretching and tertiary CN stretching vibrations [38]. Additionally, the peaks at 1289 and 1123 cm−1 are attributable to the stretching vibration of CN and CO groups within CDs [36]. The presence of these N and O functionalities endows the CDs with high hydrophilicity and therefore robust dispersity in aqueous solution, thus enables the construction of solution based fluorescent nanoprobe for certain analytes.

XPS was further employed to identify the chemical status of the CDs, from the survey spectrum shown in Fig. 3 a, the CDs are mainly composed of C, N and O elements free of other impurities, showing the carbonaceous framework of CDs with doping or decoration of N and O groups. The atomic fraction of C, N and O are 65.2, 12.9 and 21.9%, respectively, evidencing the enriched N, O functionalities onto carbon motif. To more concretely identify the elemental form, the C1s, N1s and O1s spectra were deconvoluted and fitted. As shown in Fig. 3b, the C1s spectrum of CDs mainly includes CC (284.6 eV), CN (285.5 eV), CO (286.4 eV) and CN/CO (288.3 eV) groups [23]. Moreover, the N1s spectrum can be fitted into CNC (399.3 eV) and NH bonds (400.3 eV) [23], showing the effective incorporation of N atoms into the carbonaceous framework of CDs. The high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 3d) reveals the decoration of CO (531.3 eV), COH (532.7 eV) groups onto carbonaceous framework [39]. All these results indicate the incorporation of multiple N and O groups onto CDs, which not only increase the hydrophilicity, but serve as chromophores or auxochromes stabilizing the surface active sites, therefore enhance the fluorescent activity of CDs.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of aqueous CDs solution (Fig. S1) exhibits a characteristic absorption peak at 270 nm, which is mainly derived from the π-π* transition of skeletal aromatic carbon rings. Additionally, the high background within 300–400 nm can be ascribable to the n-π* transition of the double bonds containing groups such as CO and CN [40]. Under excitation wavelengths (λexs) at 360 and 700 nm, the CDs solution demonstrates a strong fluorescence emission peak at 440 nm (Fig. 4 a), showing the typical down-conversion and up-conversion fluorescence features of CDs, these results indicate that the photoluminence (PL) of CDs can be achieved by excitation of high energy photons (down-conversion) and multi-photons with of lower energy (up-conversion). Fig. 4b,c show the λex dependent PL spectra, within the λex ranges from 200 to 290 nm (Fig. 4b), the PL intensity increases gradually with emission wavelength maintains almost unvaried at 420 nm, this section of λex independent PL feature is presumably aroused by the double bond containing functionalities such as carbonyl, amide and imine groups [33], the constant ground state-excitation state energy level difference leads to the invariable emission wavelength. Further increase the λex from 300 to 520 nm (Fig. 4c), the emission intensity increases initially and then decreases, the maximized emission at 440 nm achieves under λex of 360 nm, and the emission wavelength also red shifts gradually with λex. This portion of excitation dependent PL behavior is presumably derived from the optical selection of CDs with different surface defect states and particle sizes [41]. The dual emission rationales with similar emission wavelength together lead to the strong blue down-conversion emission behavior. Simultaneously, the CDs also exhibits weak up-conversion fluorescence feature, when excited within λex range of 530–750 nm (Fig. 4d), CDs demonstrate relatively weaker emission within 420–450 nm, the highest emission intensity was achieved at 440 nm under λex of 700 nm. This λex dependent up-conversion emission should be ascribed to the simultaneous absorption of two or more photons to sustain the transition of ground state electrons to excitation state and further the emission at shorter wavelengths [19]. A cotton thread dyed with CDs exhibits light blue, green and red fluorescence colors under excitations at λexs of 405, 488 and 540 nm, respectively (Fig. S2), more intuitively evidence the multi-colored fluorescence feature of CDs. Using quinine sulfate as a standard, the relative fluorescent QY of CDs is estimated to be 9% under λex of 360 nm, and the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs is calculated to be 4.43 ns according to mono-exponential simulation (Fig. S3), showing the strong PL property as a consequence of the N doped aromatic carbon framework enriched with abundant surface defects and the decoration of rich chromophorous groups.

The PL emission intensities of CDs solutions at different concentrations were also evaluated, under λex of 360 nm (Fig. 5 a), the PL intensity increases accordingly with CDs concentration, the emission intensity displays fine linearity with concentration of CDs up to 250 μg mL−1 without discernable self-quenching phenomenon, the strong PL behavior enables the application of CDs as fluorescent probe in quantitatively analysis of certain substance with high sensitivity. The up-conversion PL emission (under λex of 700 nm) also displays good linearity with the CDs concentration (Fig. 5b), showing the up-conversion PL can also be employed as PL probes. Considering the much higher PL intensity, the down-conversion emission feature is more preferable fluorescent sensing platform for certain guest quenchers.

The resistance against photobleaching is the traditional advantage of CDs for the photochemical inertness of carbonaceous materials, so the aqueous CDs solution was excited at 360 and 700 nm for 300 min (Fig. S4), the PL intensity of CDs solution maintains almost unvaried, suggesting the good stability against photobleaching. Moreover, the down- and up-conversion PL emission intensities of the CDs are both stable in aqueous NaCl solutions with concentrations range between 0 and 25 mM, suggesting the high stability of CDs against ionic strength, which is essential for accurate sensing of analytes in body fluid containing electrolyte. Furthermore, different inorganic ions were introduced into the aqueous CDs solution, the PL emission intensity keeps unvaried when the main group metal ions such as Mg2+, K+, Na+ and common anions e.g. Cl−, SO4 2− and NO3 − ions (100 μmol L−1 in concentration) are introduced, these ions can not quench the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 6 a). The Fe3+ ions obviously quenches the PL intensity of CDs, which may be due to the oxidation of hydroquinone functionalities and eliminates partial fluorescence of CDs [42,43], so it can be inferred that the CDs can find application in selective detection of the Fe3+ ion in aquatic environment. Besides, different biomolecules such as amino acids, DNA and glucose (2.5 μg L−1 in concentration) cause the slight reduction of PL to the same extent (Fig. 6b), hence these species can not cause discriminating interference in different body fluid samples.

The effect of pH on the down-conversion and up-conversion fluorescence spectrum of CDs was also investigated over a pH range between 2 and 11 (Fig. 6c). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs maintains at a high level in the of pH range of 4 ∼ 8 (inset), whereas decreases to different extents in acidic or alkali media with slight alteration in emission wavelength, this pH dependent PL feature is presumably due to the protonation and deprotonation of certain chromophorous groups, which decrease the PL intensity and alter the energy difference between ground state and excitation state. Because the overall PL spectrum is the superimposition of fluorescence emissions with different rationales, the slight variation in emission wavelength is also reasonable. Due to the high PL emission intensity is achieved in neutral media, neutral pH medium is the optimal environment for construction of fluorescent sensing platform. Up-conversion PL of CDs is also pH dependent (Fig. 6d), but the emission intensity is much lower, so the down-conversion fluorescence can be employed as fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of quenchers.

Given the strong PL feature in neutral medium, the strong resistance against photo-bleaching and the PL stability in different ions and biomolecules, the CDs is attempted as PL probe for detection of LH. Fig. 7 illustrated the effect of CDs concentration on fluorescence intensity of CDs-LH system with fixed LH concentration of 50 μg mL−1 (0.185 mmol L−1) at λex = 360 nm. With the increment of CDs concentration in absence of LH, the PL intensity increases linearly. After the incorporation of LH, the apparent decrement in PL intensity indicates that LH dramatically quenches the PL of CDs, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency was also found to be dependent on the CDs concentration. At higher CDs concentration, the quenching extent (ΔF = F0-F) increases, the more sensitive to LH is mainly due to the more sufficient energy transfer between LH and CDs. When the CDs concentration increases to 125 μg mL−1, ΔF is the largest. To reach a balance between the sensitivity and the measuring range of the fluorescence intensity, 125 μg mL−1 was selected as the optimum CDs concentration for LH detection.

Buffer solution is essential in maintaining a fixed pH value in neutral medium, to check the effect of buffer solution type on the detection of LH, the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs-LH system in 1.0 mL of BR buffer solution, Na2HPO4-citric acid buffer solution, Na2HPO4-NaHCO3 buffer solution and KH2PO4-K2HPO4 buffer solution (pH = 7.0) were compared. From Fig. 8 a, the BR buffer solution system displays the strongest fluorescence intensities, and the largest ΔF also indicates that the BR is the optimal buffer medium for fluorescent LH detection. The effect of BR buffer solution concentration on the ΔF of CDs-LH system was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 8b, the fluorescence intensities of CDs and CDs-LH system changed gently in the range of 0.5–2.0 mL, whereas the ΔF is the largest in 1.0 mL buffer system, so 1.0 mL of BR buffer solution was selected as optimal dosage for LH detection. Based on the abovementioned optimization trials, the CDs concentration of 125 μg mL−1 in 1.0 mL of BR buffer solution at pH = 7.0 was used as optimal medium for detection of LH.

In order to study the effect of ambient light on the fluorescence stability for LH detection, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-LH system was measured undergoes exposure to ambient light for different time course. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-LH system remains unvaried after exposure to ambient light for at least 2 h. The fluorescence spectra of CDs-LH system were recorded by eleven parallel trials. The emission intensities are almost equal, and the 0.14% relative standard deviation ensures good reproducibility of CDs probe for practical LH analysis (Fig. S6).

The PL spectra of CDs by increasing the concentrations of LH were determined under excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 9 a). LH dramatically quenches the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs without altering the maximum emission wavelength. The efficient quenching effect suggests that a high affinity between LH and the chromophorous site of CDs, the change in chemical microenvironment of CDs inhibits the PL emission. The PL intensity decreases accordingly with LH concentration, showing the good potential of CDs as credible fluorescence probe for quantitatively detection of LH. From Fig. 9b, the fluorescence emission of CDs-LH system varies linearly with the concentration of LH within 0.185 ∼ 1.295 mmol L−1. The linear range is comparable to the traditional HPLC method, whereas inferior to the Ag nanoparticles colorimetric probes and potentiometric sensor, Table S1 compares the major analytic parameters of different techniques for LH detection. The regression equation was: △F = 57.39 + 38.80 × 104 [Q] (mol L−1) with regression coefficient of 0.997 (n = 7) and a detection limit of 0.054 mmol L−1 (n = 11).

The effects of saccharides, cellucose, DNA, urea acid, and common inorganic ions on the fluorescent intensity of CDs-LH system were also evaluated. As shown in Table 1 , the fluorescence intensity of CDs-LH system changes slightly by the introduction of these foreign substances. From the pharmacokinetics perspective, the reduction of the fluorescence intensity by these foreign substances means the destruction of CDs-LH system in actual blood sample, which will shorten the storage time of CDs-LH specimen. Hence, the negligible interference from foreign substances in the detection of LH implies that the fluorescence probe can be potentially used for LH detection in practical body fluid samples allowing longer storage time.

Generally, the fluorescence quenching mechanism by colorless quenchers can be classified into static quenching and dynamic quenching. In the static quenching mechanism, the fluorophores and quencher forms ground state complex, the interaction between them does not change the chemical structure and activity of fluorophores. Whereas for dynamic quench mechanism, the interaction between quencher and fluorophores commonly creates a non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent ground state, which relies on the diffusional collision between them [44]. To get in-depth insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism by LH, the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs-LH system was analyzed according to Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)) at 298 K [45].
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
Where the F0 and F represent the PL intensities in the absence and presence of LH, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Kq is the bimolecular quenching rate constant and τ 0 is the lifetime of the fluorophore in the absence of quencher. [Q] is the concentration of LH quencher. According to Eq. (1), the F0 /F versus [Q] was plotted (Fig. 10 a). The regression equation was F0 /F = 0.863 + 0.112 × 104 [Q] (R2 = 0.992, n = 7). The Ksv was yielded to be 0.112 × 104 L mol−1 from the slope of the regression straightline. Based on the fluorescence lifetime of 4.43 × 10−9 s, the bimolecular quenching rate constant was calculated to be 2.5 × 1011 L mol−1 s−1 by Ksv:τ 0 ratio. This value is obviously higher over the limiting diffusion constant Kdif of the biomolecule (Kdif = 2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1) [46], which suggests that the fluorescence quenching was induced by specific interaction between CDs and LH, and the quenching was mainly arisen from static quenching by the formation of a ground state complex [47].

In term of static quenching process, the number of binding site (n) and the binding constant (K) can be obtained according to Eq. (2) (Fig. 10b) [48]:
(2)
l g
F 0
− F
F
= l g k +
n l
g
[ Q ]
The binding constant K was 0.06 L mol−1 and the number of binding site was obtained as n = 1.21 (R2 = 0.998). The moderate binding affinity of CDs to LH suggests the formation of static CDs-LH complex, the energy transfer between them finally causes the fluorescence quenching of CDs.

The cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed by the MTT assay using PC-12 cells as representative model, as shown in Fig. 11 , over 90% of the PC-12 cells remain alive after being incubated with CDs dose up to 400 μg mL−1 for 24 h, showing the low cytotoxicity of CDs. Hence, the CDs can be potentially applied as safe fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of LH in practical body fluid samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of LH in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability and reliability of the proposed fluorescent CDs nanoprobe for detection of LH pharmaceutical practical medium, medical LH injection was spiked into different medium, as shown in Table 2 , three different concentrations of LH injection were spiked into the human serum, and the concentration detected by the CDs probe are extremely close to the values found in neutral buffer solution, evidencing the applicability of the proposed fluorescent probe for accurately determination of LH pharmaceutical in practical body fluid. The fluorescent CDs sensing platform can also be applied for LH detection in aquatic environments such as lake water, tap water, and well water with the similar recovery ratios, showing the wide applicability for LH analysis. Given the low cost and simplicity in preparation of CDs, the low detection limits in determination of LH, as well as the excellent recovery results in LH detection in human serum, the proposed fluorescent CDs sensing platform is a feasible method for selective detection of LH in pharmaceutical industry and clinical practice.

In summary, CDs with down-conversion and up-conversion fluorescent features were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of fish scales, the as-prepared CDs demonstrated strong PL emission with QY of 9%, robust resistance against photobleaching and interference substances, which enable the application as fluorescence probe for quantitative detection of LH with a high linear range and a low detection limit by virtue of the statically quenching of the fluorescence of CDs.
Authors declare no competing interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by NSFC (No. 21671059), Program for Innovative Research Team and Individuals in University of Henan Province (Nos. 18 IRTSTHN003 and 15HASTIT006) and the 111 Project (No. D17007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.079.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Systemic lidocaine for neuropathic pain relief
Long-term treatment of neuropathic pain with a 5% lidocaine medicated plaster
Site of action and active form of local anesthetics
The molecular structure and vibrational, 1H and 13C NMR spectra of lidocaine hydrochloride monohydrate

Validation of liquid chromatographic method for analysis of lidocaine hydrochloride dexamethasone acetate, calcium dobesilate, buthylhydroxyanisol and degradation product hydroquinone in suppositories and ointment
Simultaneous determination of lidocaine hydrochloride, hydrocortisone and nystatin in a pharmaceutical preparation by RP-LC
Decreasing analysis time in capillary electrophoresis: validation and comparison of quantitative performances in several approaches
Validation of capillary electrophoresis-mass spectrometry methods for the analysis of a pharmaceutical formulation
Synthesis of highly fluorescent gold nanoparticles for sensing mercury(II)
Coumarin-derived Cu2+-selective fluorescence sensor: synthesis, mechanisms, and applications in living cells
CdTe quantum dots (QDs) based kinetic discrimination of Fe2+ and Fe3+, and CdTe QDs-fenton hybrid system for sensitive photoluminescent detection of Fe2+
Ligand replacement-induced fluorescence switch of quantum dots for ultrasensitive detection of organophosphorothioate pesticides
Charge-transfer-induced fluorescence quenching of anthracene derivatives and selective detection of picric acid
Conjugated microporous polymers-based fluorescein for fluorescence detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
Activatable QD-based near-infrared fluorescence probe for sensitive detection and imaging of DNA
Highly sensitive and selective bifunctional oligonucleotide probe for homogeneous parallel fluorescence detection of protein and nucleotide sequence
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Photoluminescence-tunable carbon nanodots: surface-state energy-gap tuning
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Green and economical synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from vegetables for sensing and imaging applications
Carrot-derived carbon dots modified with polyethyleneimine and nile blue for ratiometric two-photon fluorescence turn-on sensing of sulfide anion in biological fluids
Label-free fluorimetric detection of CEA using carbon dots derived from tomato juice
Facile synthesis of red-emitting carbon dots from pulp-free lemon juice for bioimaging
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon dots for bioimaging from Bombyx mori silk-natural proteins
Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots sensors for nitric oxide fluorescence quantification
A novel porous nanocomposite of sulfur/carbon obtained from fish scales for lithium-sulfur batteries
A fish scale based hierarchical lamellar porous carbon material obtained using a natural template for high performance electrochemical capacitors
Solvent-free synthesis of sulfur-and nitrogen-co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from glutathione for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercury(II) ions
Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Fluorescent carbon quantum dots, capacitance and catalysis active porous carbon microspheres from beer
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur-and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence
Preparation of functionalized water-soluble photoluminescent carbon quantum dots from petroleum coke
Chemical and structural properties of carbonaceous products obtained by hydrothermal carbonization of saccharides
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbonnanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescence probe suitable for sensing Fe3+ under acidic conditions
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron (III) ions and dopamine
Turn-off fluorescence of amino functionalized carbon quantum dots as effective fluorescent probes for determination of isotretinoin
Spectroscopic and molecular docking studies of binding interaction of gefitinib, lapatinib and sunitinib withbovine serum albumin (BSA)
Exploring the interaction of silver nanoparticles with lysozyme: binding behaviors and kinetics
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped cardon dots with strong blue luminescence
Fluorescent carbon dots for glyphosate determination based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer and logic gate operation

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yi Zhang obtained her BS and MS degrees in analytical chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Henan Normal University. She is currently an associate professor in School of Laboratory Medicine at Xinxiang Medical University, She is currently studying for her PhD in inorganic chemistry at Henan Normal University.
Zhiyong Gao is an associate professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Henan Normal University. His current interests mainly focus on carbon quantum dots.
Weiqun Zhang is a laboratory physician in The Third Affiliated Hospital of Xinxiang Medical University.
Wei Wang is a labortory physician in The Third Affiliated Hospital of Xinxiang Medical University.
Jiuli Chang obtained her PhD degree in inorganic chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Henan Normal University. Her current interests mainly focus on carbon materials.
Kai Jiang is a professor in School of Environment at Henan Normal University. His current interests mainly focus on environmental analytical chemistry and environmental materials.
